items.title: Executive order on advanced AI and cybersecurity signed
items.summary: President Trump signed an executive order directing federal agencies to strengthen cyber defenses and create a voluntary framework for reviewing frontier AI models.
items.details: On June 2, 2026, the White House issued an order titled 'Promoting Advanced Artificial Intelligence Innovation and Security.' It directs agencies to bolster cyber defense of government systems and establishes a process for developers to voluntarily provide the government early access to frontier AI models before release.

items.title: Executive order reclassifies federal workforce positions
items.summary: President Trump signed an executive order reclassifying about 8,000 senior policy-influencing federal positions into a category called Schedule Policy/Career.
items.details: According to the White House, the order reclassifies roughly 8,000 senior policy-influencing positions into Schedule Policy/Career. The administration states these remain career positions, that hiring processes will not change, and that removal decisions will be made without respect to political affiliation.

items.title: President signs Secure America Act into law
items.summary: President Trump signed the Secure America Act, providing funding for immigration enforcement agencies through fiscal year 2029.
items.details: On June 10, 2026, President Trump signed S. 2, which provides funding to the Department of Homeland Security, Customs and Border Protection, and Immigration and Customs Enforcement through fiscal year 2029. The bill allocates $69.5 billion to ICE and CBP and was passed using the budget reconciliation process.

items.title: Trump announces 60-day ceasefire extension with Iran
items.summary: President Trump announced a further 60-day ceasefire in the 2026 Iran war to allow negotiations toward a final agreement.
items.details: On June 11, 2026, Trump announced a 60-day ceasefire extension intended to allow parties to negotiate contentious issues. The announcement followed renewed exchanges of fire after Israeli strikes in Lebanon and disputes over the Strait of Hormuz. Trump also said he had called off threatened new military strikes on Iran.

items.title: World Bank cuts global growth forecast citing Iran war
items.summary: The World Bank lowered its forecast for global economic growth, citing fallout from the Iran war.
items.details: On June 11, 2026, the World Bank cut its forecast for global economic growth, citing the fallout from the Iran war. The same day, U.S. producer prices spiked in May driven by higher energy prices, the largest yearly jump since 2022, amid disruptions to traffic through the Strait of Hormuz.

items.title: Congress passes Secure America Act funding immigration enforcement
items.summary: The House and Senate passed S. 2, providing immigration enforcement funding through fiscal year 2029 using the reconciliation process.
items.details: The Senate passed S. 2 on June 5 and the House passed it on June 9, 2026. The bill provides funding to DHS, CBP, and ICE through fiscal year 2029 for immigration enforcement. It was advanced through budget reconciliation, requiring only a simple majority in each chamber.

items.title: House passes Ukraine Support Act via discharge petition
items.summary: The House passed H.R. 2913, the Ukraine Support Act, in a 226-195 vote after a discharge petition forced the vote.
items.details: On June 4, 2026, the House passed the Ukraine Support Act 226-195, with 18 Republicans and one independent joining Democrats. The bill authorizes military and reconstruction assistance for Ukraine and establishes sanctions on Russia. It was brought to the floor through a discharge petition that gathered 218 signatures. The measure now moves to the Senate.

items.title: Supreme Court allows Alabama to use 2023 congressional map
items.summary: The Supreme Court cleared the way for Alabama to use a congressional map in the 2026 elections that a lower court had found racially discriminatory.
items.details: On June 2, 2026, the Supreme Court issued an unsigned 6-3 order allowing Alabama to use its 2023 congressional map for the 2026 elections, after a lower court had barred it. Justice Sotomayor dissented, joined by Justices Kagan and Jackson. The map is likely to reduce the number of Democratic-held seats in the state from two to one.

items.title: Supreme Court issues decisions in multiple cases
items.summary: The Supreme Court released opinions in several cases as it approached its summer recess, including FCC v. AT&T.
items.details: On June 4, 2026, the Court ruled in FCC v. AT&T that the Federal Communications Commission may issue forfeiture orders without a jury without offending the Seventh Amendment. The Court also decided Sripetch v. SEC and Hikma Pharmaceuticals v. Amarin Pharma the same day, with additional opinions issued June 11.

items.title: Louisiana enacts new congressional map after court ruling
items.summary: Louisiana enacted a new congressional map eliminating one of the state's two majority-Black districts following the Supreme Court's Callais decision.
items.details: Gov. Jeff Landry signed the new map as Act 2 of the 2026 Regular Session after the Legislature passed Senate Bill 121. The redraw responded to the Supreme Court's ruling in Louisiana v. Callais and could shift the state's U.S. House delegation from a 4-2 to a 5-1 Republican-Democrat split. A federal court later postponed a hearing on a challenge to the map.

items.title: California holds statewide primary election
items.summary: California held its statewide primary on June 2, 2026, with candidates advancing in the open race for governor and other offices.
items.details: Under California's top-two primary system, Democrat Xavier Becerra and Republican Steve Hilton advanced to the November general election for governor, succeeding term-limited Gov. Gavin Newsom. The June 2 ballot included races for governor, other statewide offices, all 52 U.S. House seats, and state legislative seats.

items.title: South Carolina redistricting effort fails in Senate
items.summary: A Trump-backed effort to redraw South Carolina's congressional map ahead of the 2026 midterms ended after the state Senate declined to advance it.
items.details: On May 26, 2026, the South Carolina Senate voted 24-20 against ending debate on a new congressional map, with 12 Republicans joining 12 Democrats. The House had passed a map aimed at the seat held by Rep. James Clyburn, but the Senate then adjourned the special session, ending the effort before the June 9 primary.

CA.title: Newsom signs election interference protection law
CA.summary: Gov. Gavin Newsom signed Senate Bill 73, strengthening California's election safeguards against interference and intimidation ahead of the June primary.
CA.details: On May 27, 2026, Newsom signed SB 73, which the state describes as adding protections for voters and election workers and erecting barriers against unauthorized federal access to voting systems, voter rolls, and polling places. The law was signed ahead of California's June 2 statewide primary.
CA.source: Office of the Governor of California

LA.title: Gov. Landry signs new congressional map
LA.summary: Gov. Jeff Landry signed a new congressional map that eliminates one of Louisiana's two majority-Black districts.
LA.details: The map, enacted as Act 2 of the 2026 Regular Session via Senate Bill 121, was drawn after the U.S. Supreme Court struck down the prior map in Louisiana v. Callais. It is expected to elect five Republicans and one Democrat. Landry had earlier postponed the state's May 16 U.S. House primary.

NY.title: Legislature passes redistricting constitutional amendment
NY.summary: New York's legislature gave first passage to a constitutional amendment that would allow mid-decade congressional redistricting beginning in 2028.
NY.details: On June 3-4, 2026, the Democratic-led legislature passed an amendment that would permit mid-decade redistricting and remove constitutional language barring gerrymandering. It must pass again next year and be approved by voters in 2027 before taking effect, meaning it would not affect the 2026 midterms.
NY.source: Spectrum News / NY State of Politics

FL.title: DeSantis signs additional bills into law
FL.summary: Gov. Ron DeSantis signed another batch of bills into law in mid-June, adding to more than 160 laws enacted in 2026.
FL.details: Across June 10-11, DeSantis signed 25 additional bills, many addressing local matters such as county districts and special districts. Earlier, the Legislature passed a DeSantis-backed constitutional amendment (HJR 1-F) proposing an expanded homestead property tax exemption for the November 2026 ballot.

TX.title: Gov. Abbott announces grants and appointments
TX.summary: Gov. Greg Abbott announced over $26 million in grant funding for infrastructure projects in military communities.
TX.details: The funds were announced through the Texas Military Preparedness Commission's Defense Economic Adjustment Assistance Grant program. Abbott also announced state board appointments and opened nominations for the 2026 Star of Texas Awards, due to the governor's office by June 15, 2026.

MI.title: Whitmer re-establishes gun violence prevention task force
MI.summary: Gov. Gretchen Whitmer re-established the state's Gun Violence Prevention Task Force.
MI.details: In a June 4, 2026 announcement, Whitmer's office said she re-established the task force, building on prior state efforts. Whitmer, who is term-limited, also made appointments to state boards and commissions the same day.

IA.title: Gov. Reynolds signs final 2026 session bills
IA.summary: Gov. Kim Reynolds signed the final bills from the 2026 legislative session, the last she will sign as governor.
IA.details: On June 2, 2026, Reynolds signed dozens of measures, the final bills of the session. Among earlier-signed laws she highlighted were property tax changes, charter school and Education Savings Account measures, and pediatric cancer research funding. Reynolds is not seeking another term in 2026.

CT.title: Gov. Lamont signs omnibus solar energy bill
CT.summary: Gov. Ned Lamont signed HB 5340, an energy policy bill extending renewable energy programs while placing new limits on solar development.
CT.details: The law extends Connecticut's Renewable Energy Solutions programs through 2035, launches a community solar program, requires automated residential solar permitting, and allows balcony solar. It also places a moratorium on certain large-scale solar development and caps annual funding for new installations.

VA.title: Redistricting fight ends, leaving 2021 map in place
VA.summary: Litigation over Virginia's redistricting referendum ended, leaving the congressional districts adopted in 2021 in place for 2026.
VA.details: The Supreme Court of Virginia struck down the referendum 4-3 on May 8, finding the legislature did not follow required procedures, and the U.S. Supreme Court declined to revive it. Several Democratic candidates suspended campaigns. Virginia's existing map stays in place, with primaries scheduled for Aug. 4.

AL.title: State to use 2023 congressional map for 2026
AL.summary: Alabama will use its 2023 congressional map in the 2026 elections after the U.S. Supreme Court cleared its use.
AL.details: On June 2, 2026, the Supreme Court allowed Alabama to use the 2023 map that a lower court had found racially discriminatory. The state will use the map originally drawn in 2023, which is likely to reduce the number of Democratic-held House seats in the state from two to one.

SC.title: Senate ends redistricting special session
SC.summary: The South Carolina Senate declined to advance a new congressional map, ending the state's redistricting effort before the June 9 primary.
SC.details: On May 26, 2026, the Senate voted 24-20 against ending debate on the map, with 12 Republicans joining 12 Democrats. The House had passed a map targeting Rep. James Clyburn's district, but the Senate adjourned the special session, which Gov. Henry McMaster had convened.

OH.title: Property tax relief credit begins reaching homeowners
OH.summary: Ohio property owners are set to begin seeing relief from a previously enacted property tax measure as early as June 2026.
OH.details: House Bill 186, signed into law in late 2025, establishes an Inflation Cap Credit limiting school district property tax increases to the rate of inflation, which the Ohio House said would save property owners nearly $1.7 billion over three years. The bill was among five property tax measures enacted by the state.

disclaimer: This digest reports verifiable recent developments from cited sources and takes no position on any party, official, or policy.
